summary_text:
S:
The patient reports fatigue for the past two months and reports pain in his wrist. He states the pain is like a nine on a scale of one to ten. The patient reports that the pain goes away when he takes ibuprofen. The patient feels the pain most of the time and it is worse when his arm is raised and elevated. The patient feels better when his arm is kept straight. The patient reports tingling, unsure if it's due to swelling.

O:
The clinician palpates the wrist and finds tenderness. The clinician observes normal range of movement with the right wrist and positive abduction adduction pain on movement. The clinician observes obvious swelling and bruising. Elevated blood pressure was noted. The patient's heart rate is elevated at approximately eighty beats per minute. The patient's respiratory rate is normal at twenty breaths per minute. A positive colles' fracture was identified. Dorsal angulation of the distal fracture fragment is present. Ulnar styloid fracture is present.

A:
The clinician suspects cervical radiculopathy and plans a cervical MRI. The patient's pain is primarily along his wrist and does not extend very far. The patient bumped his wrist yesterday morning.

P:
The patient will receive a thumb spica brace for today. The clinician will prescribe fifty milligrams of ultram every six hours. The clinician will prescribe physical therapy a few weeks after surgery. The surgery will be performed as quickly as possible. The patient will stay overnight after surgery. The patient will wear the brace for approximately six weeks. The patient will attend physical therapy three times a week. The fracture appears extra-articular and proximal to the radial ulnar joint. S The patient reports fatigue for the past two months and reports pain in his wrist. He states the pain is like a nine on a scale of one to ten. The patient reports that the pain goes away when he takes ibuprofen. The patient feels the pain most of the time and it is worse when his arm is raised and elevated. The patient feels better when his arm is kept straight. The patient reports tingling, unsure if it's due to swelling. O The clinician palpates the wrist and finds tenderness. The clinician observes normal range of movement with the right wrist and positive abduction adduction pain on movement. The clinician observes obvious swelling and bruising. Elevated blood pressure was noted. The patient's heart rate is elevated at approximately eighty beats per minute. The patient's respiratory rate is normal at twenty breaths per minute. A positive colles' fracture was identified. Dorsal angulation of the distal fracture fragment is present. Ulnar styloid fracture is present. reference_summary:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Right wrist injury.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Diana Scott is a pleasant 61-year-old female who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of a right wrist injury. The patient sustained this injury yesterday morning, 05/12/2022, when she slipped on the stairs while carrying a laundry basket. She states she tried to catch herself with her arms outstretched. The patient reports an immediate onset of pain and swelling in her right wrist. She denies any previous injuries to her right arm. The patient rates her pain level as a 9/10. Her pain is aggravated by movement. The patient also reports numbness and tingling in her fingers. She has been icing and wrapping her right wrist with an ACE wrap. The patient has also been taking ibuprofen, which provides some relief. She notes fully extending her arm while resting it on a pillow alleviates some of her pain as well.

The patient is going on vacation in 1 month.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports right wrist pain and swelling.
Neurological: Reports numbness and tingling to the digits of the right hand.

VITALS

Blood Pressure: Elevated at 140/70 mmHg.
Heart Rate: 80 beats per minute.
Respiratory Rate: 20 breaths per minute.
Body Temperature: 97.2 degrees F.
HEAD: Normocephalic
NECK: No swelling noted
CV: No bilateral lower extremity edema. No carotid bruit. No murmurs, gallops or rubs heard during auscultation of the heart. Palpabale pulses to the bilateral lower extremities.
RESPIRATORY: Normal respiratory effort no respiratory distress. Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally.
GI/GU: Non-distended
BACK: No evidence of trauma or deformity
NECK: No adenopathy. No thyromegaly.
MSK: Examination of the right wrist: Limited range of movement. Tenderness to palpation. Pain on flexion and extension. Pain with radial deviation and lateral deviation. Pain with wrist abduction and adduction. The metacarpals are intact. Obvious swelling and bruising. Tenderness on palpation throughout. There is evidence of potential fracture feeling and bony crepitus.

RESULTS

X-rays of the right wrist were taken today. These reveal the fracture appears extra-articular and proximal to the radioulnar joint. Dorsal angulation of the distal fracture fragment is present to a variable degree. Dorsal angulation is severe, presenting with a dinner fork deformity. An ulnar styloid fracture is present.

ASSESSMENT

Right wrist Colles fracture.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have discussed with the patient that her x-rays revealed a right wrist Colles fracture. We discussed treatment options and I have recommended that we proceed with a right wrist ORIF and all indicated procedures. We reviewed the risks, benefits, and alternatives of the surgery. I explained that we should schedule the operation for as soon as possible to ensure adequate healing and to limit malformation of the wrist. I advised that this procedure will require her to be admitted to the hospital for an overnight stay.

In the meantime, the patient will be placed in a thumb spica brace and receive instructions on its proper usage. I have prescribed the patient Ultram 50 mg every 6 hours to treat her pain. We also discussed that her postoperative course will include wearing a brace for 6 weeks. A few weeks after surgery, the patient will initiate formal physical therapy and will attend 3 times per week to strengthen her right wrist. The patient has a 2-week vacation planned in 1 month. I recommended that we consult with her physical therapist to determine if there are exercises she can perform on her own instead of postponing her vacation.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me in 4 weeks status post surgery to discuss her progress.

section_miscategorizations.text: The clinician suspects cervical radiculopathy and plans a cervical MRI.
section_miscategorizations.reason: The diagnostic impression (suspects cervical radiculopathy) belongs in Assessment, but the MRI is an order and should be documented in the Plan. Keeping the imaging order only in A obscures the management plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: The fracture appears extra-articular and proximal to the radial ulnar joint.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This is a descriptive diagnostic characterization of the fracture, part of the clinician's assessment, not a treatment or management action.

section_miscategorizations.text: S The patient reports fatigue for the past two months and reports pain in his wrist. He states the pain is like a nine on a scale of one to ten. The patient reports that the pain goes away when he takes ibuprofen. The patient feels the pain most of the time and it is worse when his arm is raised and elevated. The patient feels better when his arm is kept straight. The patient reports tingling, unsure if it's due to swelling.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This entire block is patient-reported symptom history and belongs in the Subjective section, not in the Plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: O The clinician palpates the wrist and finds tenderness. The clinician observes normal range of movement with the right wrist and positive abduction adduction pain on movement. The clinician observes obvious swelling and bruising. Elevated blood pressure was noted. The patient's heart rate is elevated at approximately eighty beats per minute. The patient's respiratory rate is normal at twenty breaths per minute. A positive colles' fracture was identified. Dorsal angulation of the distal fracture fragment is present. Ulnar styloid fracture is present.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This block contains exam findings and diagnostic imaging/observation results, which are Objective data, not elements of the management Plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: A The clinician suspects cervical radiculopathy and plans a cervical MRI. The patient's pain is primarily along his wrist and does not extend very far. The patient bumped his wrist yesterday morning.
section_miscategorizations.reason: This repeated block in the Plan section mixes assessment (suspected diagnosis) and subjective history (mechanism of injury, pain distribution). Neither belongs in the Plan; the suspicion should be in Assessment and the history in Subjective.
